<!-- Topic 5: Enumerations -->
<!-- Slides 46-57 -->

# Enumerations
<!-- Slide 46 -->

## What Are The Legal Choices? {.smaller}

- Some variables should hold one option from a small set.
- Menu choices, states, and categories are common examples.

::: notes
Slides 46-57
:::

<!-- Slide 47 -->

---


## Why Not Just Use Numbers?

- Numbers are compact but easy to misread.
- `1`, `2`, and `3` do not explain what state the program is in.
- Named choices make conditions and dispatch logic easier to read.

<!-- Slide 48 -->

---


## Why enum Instead Of Separate Constants?

- Separate constants give individual values names.
- An enum groups related choices under one concept.
- That grouping signals that the values belong to the same legal set.

<!-- Slide 49 -->

---


## Basic enum Syntax

```cpp
enum MenuChoice {
    ADD_STUDENT = 1,
    PRINT_ROSTER = 2,
    QUIT = 0
};
```

<!-- Slide 50 -->

---


## Enumerator Values

- Enumerator names map to integer values.
- Values can be assigned explicitly.
- If no value is assigned, C++ counts upward from the previous value.

<!-- Slide 51 -->

---


## Using enum Values

```cpp
MenuChoice choice = ADD_STUDENT;

if (choice == ADD_STUDENT) {
    cout << "Add student" << endl;
}
```

<!-- Slide 52 -->

---


## enum With switch

```cpp
switch (choice) {
case ADD_STUDENT:
    cout << "Add student" << endl;
    break;
case PRINT_ROSTER:
    cout << "Print roster" << endl;
    break;
case QUIT:
    cout << "Goodbye" << endl;
    break;
}
```

<!-- Slide 53 -->

---


## Input Still Needs Translation

- Console input does not arrive as an enum.
- Read the client input first.
- Validate the raw input, then translate it into the named choice.

<!-- Slide 54 -->

---


## A Small Translation Step

```cpp
int rawChoice;
cin >> rawChoice;

while (rawChoice != ADD_STUDENT &&
       rawChoice != PRINT_ROSTER &&
       rawChoice != QUIT) {
    cout << "Choose 1, 2, or 0: ";
    cin >> rawChoice;
}

MenuChoice choice = static_cast<MenuChoice>(rawChoice);
```

<!-- Slide 55 -->

---


## When enum Fits

- Use enum for closed sets of named choices.
- Avoid enum for open-ended quantities.
- Avoid enum for arbitrary text values.

<!-- Slide 56 -->

---


## Summary

- Enumerations replace mystery values with named choices.
- They work best after validation has limited the value to the legal set.

<!-- Slide 57 -->
